task: I’m trying to turn one brand concept into a few different social posts without making each version feel like a full redesign. What’s the simplest way to set that up so I can move fast and keep the visuals consistent?

turns.agent_response:
The simplest setup is to build **one “master post system”** and then make small, repeatable swaps instead of redesigning each post.

### Use a fixed template structure
Create a base layout with:
- **Same grid and spacing**
- **Same type hierarchy**  
  - headline
  - subhead
  - CTA or footer
- **Same brand elements**  
  - colors
  - logo placement
  - shapes/textures
  - image treatment
- **One or two flexible content zones**  
  - headline
  - image
  - accent color
  - icon/quote/stat

### Make only a few variables change
For each version, keep everything constant except:
- the main message
- one supporting visual
- maybe a background color or crop
- optional CTA wording

### Build a mini system, not one-off posts
Think in terms of:
- **1 core template**
- **3–5 variations**
- **1 rules sheet** for what can change

Example rules:
- Headline always top-left
- Logo always bottom-right
- Use only 3 brand colors
- Photos always use the same filter or overlay
- No new fonts, no new shapes

### Best workflow
1. Design the master template
2. Define the editable fields
3. Duplicate it for each post
4. Swap only the approved variables
5. Save as a reusable library

### If you want to move even faster
Set up:
- **text styles**
- **color styles**
- **component blocks**
- **pre-cropped image placeholders**

That way, every new post feels like a controlled version of the same brand idea, not a fresh design.

If you want, I can also give you a **simple social post template framework** you can use in Figma, Canva, or Adobe Express.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right lane, but it’s a little generic. I don’t need the theory — I need the actual system that keeps the thing looking expensive while I can crank variants fast.

Give me a tighter framework:
- what stays locked every post
- what 3 things I’m allowed to swap
- what makes a version feel ‘same campaign’ instead of ‘new design’

And if you can, make it specific for a social carousel / feed post setup. That’s the part I’m trying to streamline.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the **practical system**.

## The setup: one campaign, many posts

Think of every post as having:

- **Locked brand shell**
- **3 swap zones**
- **Repeatable campaign cues**

That’s what keeps it feeling premium instead of random.

---

## 1) What stays locked every post

These should **never change** inside the campaign:

### A. Layout structure
Pick one grid and stick to it:
- same margins
- same alignment
- same text placement logic
- same hierarchy on every slide

Example:
- headline always in the same corner/zone
- body copy always same width
- CTA always same location

### B. Typography system
Lock:
- 1 headline font
- 1 supporting font
- same weights
- same line spacing
- same max line length

No font swapping per post. That’s where things start feeling like new designs.

### C. Brand frame
Keep these fixed:
- logo position
- footer / page number treatment
- border or frame style
- recurring shape language
- texture / grain / overlay treatment
- icon style

### D. Color rules
Don’t change the full palette.
Instead:
- 1 primary brand color
- 1 neutral base
- 1 accent color used consistently

If you want variation, shift **which one is dominant**, not the palette itself.

### E. Image treatment
All visuals should share the same:
- crop style
- filter / tone
- contrast level
- background treatment
- shadow / outline rules

If one post is crisp and another is soft and washed, it stops reading as one campaign.

---

## 2) The 3 things you’re allowed to swap

This is the key to speed.

### 1. The message
Change:
- headline
- subhead
- stat
- quote
- CTA copy

This is the main variable.

### 2. The visual subject
Swap one of:
- hero image
- product angle
- illustration
- icon
- screenshot
- abstract graphic

But keep the **same treatment** and framing.

### 3. One accent variable
Only one of these should change per version:
- accent color
- background tone
- crop position
- highlight shape
- callout badge

That gives you variation without breaking the system.

---

## 3) What makes it feel like “same campaign”

A post feels like part of the same campaign when it repeats **recognizable cues**.

### Use the same:
- headline structure
- layout rhythm
- visual ratio of text to image
- color hierarchy
- type scale
- icon style
- framing device

### Repetition is the brand signal
For example:
- every slide uses the same top-left headline block
- every slide has the same footer bar
- every slide uses the same accent underline
- every slide uses the same image crop style

Even if the content changes, the viewer feels:
> “Oh, this is that series.”

---

## 4) Best structure for a carousel / feed post

Here’s the cleanest setup.

### Slide 1: Hook
Purpose: stop scroll  
Keep:
- bold headline
- strongest visual
- minimal copy

### Slides 2–4: Proof / explanation
Purpose: build the idea  
Keep:
- same layout structure
- smaller text blocks
- one message per slide

### Final slide: CTA / close
Purpose: action  
Keep:
- same footer/logo
- clear CTA
- consistent brand frame

---

## 5) A simple carousel system you can reuse

### Locked template
- 1080x1350 or 1080x1080
- fixed margins
- fixed logo placement
- fixed footer
- fixed type styles
- fixed image treatment
- fixed shape language

### Swap zones
- headline
- supporting visual
- one accent element

### Campaign rule
- every slide must share at least **2 of 3**:
  - layout
  - color logic
  - image treatment

That’s usually enough to keep it cohesive.

---

## 6) The “expensive” look comes from restraint

What makes it feel premium is not more design — it’s **fewer decisions**:
- fewer fonts
- fewer colors
- fewer layout changes
- fewer shape styles
- fewer image treatments

The more consistent the system, the more polished it feels.

---

## 7) Fast workflow

If you want to move quickly:

1. Build one master carousel template
2. Define the 3 swap zones
3. Create 3–5 post variants
4. Keep the same shell across all of them
5. Only change the message, subject, and one accent

---

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page carousel template spec** you can hand to a designer or build directly in Figma/Canva.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. Better. But I need it even more operational than that.

Give me the actual **campaign lock system** in a format I can use:

- **Locked:** 5–7 things that never move
- **Variable:** exactly 3 slots, with examples for each
- **Campaign glue:** the 3–4 cues that make every post read as the same set
- **Carousel structure:** what changes from slide 1 to 5 without breaking the system

I’m not looking for a design lecture. I want a clean spec I can build once and duplicate without thinking. If you can, make it look like something I could hand to a junior designer and they wouldn’t freestyle it.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the **campaign lock system** as a clean working spec.

---

# Campaign Lock System

## Locked: never change these 7 things
These stay identical across every post in the campaign.

1. **Canvas size**
   - Example: 1080x1350 for feed, 1080x1920 for story

2. **Grid + margins**
   - Same outer margins
   - Same content width
   - Same alignment rules

3. **Type system**
   - 1 headline font
   - 1 support font
   - fixed sizes / weights / line spacing

4. **Brand frame**
   - Logo placement
   - Footer treatment
   - Page number / slide marker style
   - Border or frame if used

5. **Color base**
   - Same core palette
   - Same background logic
   - Same text color rules

6. **Image treatment**
   - Same crop ratio
   - Same filter / tone / contrast
   - Same shadow / overlay rules

7. **Shape language**
   - Same set of shapes
   - Same corner radius
   - Same stroke weight
   - Same texture or grain if used

---

## Variable: exactly 3 slots
Only these three things are allowed to change.

### 1. Message slot
What changes:
- headline
- subhead
- stat
- CTA copy

Examples:
- “Why most teams waste time on content”
- “3 ways to speed up your workflow”
- “Start with one master system”

### 2. Visual slot
What changes:
- hero image
- product crop
- illustration
- screenshot
- icon or object

Examples:
- laptop mockup
- portrait
- close-up product shot
- UI screenshot
- abstract graphic

### 3. Accent slot
What changes:
- one accent color
- one highlight shape
- one background tint
- one callout badge
- one crop emphasis

Examples:
- blue accent instead of green
- circle badge instead of underline
- darker background on one version
- left-cropped image instead of centered

Rule: **only one accent choice per post**.

---

## Campaign glue: what makes it read as the same set
These are the recurring cues that signal “same campaign.”

1. **Same headline placement**
   - Example: always top-left or always centered

2. **Same footer or marker system**
   - Example: logo + page number + short campaign tag in the same spot

3. **Same visual treatment**
   - Example: every image has the same tone, grain, and crop style

4. **Same recurring shape or rule**
   - Example: same underline, same corner block, same frame, same highlight bar

If 3 of these 4 repeat, the set will feel unified.

---

# Carousel structure: slide-by-slide system

## Slide 1 — Hook
Purpose: stop the scroll

Keep:
- strongest headline
- largest visual
- most contrast
- least copy

Use:
- one idea only
- one focal point
- one clear campaign cue

---

## Slide 2 — Expand
Purpose: explain the idea

Keep:
- same layout shell
- same type styles
- same footer/logo
- same image treatment

Change:
- supporting message
- secondary visual crop or detail
- one accent element

---

## Slide 3 — Proof
Purpose: make it feel credible

Keep:
- same grid
- same hierarchy
- same brand frame

Change:
- stat, quote, example, or benefit
- supporting image or screenshot
- accent slot if needed

---

## Slide 4 — Detail or breakdown
Purpose: add value

Keep:
- same template structure
- same typography
- same campaign glue

Change:
- one point per slide
- one visual per slide
- one accent only if necessary

---

## Slide 5 — Close / CTA
Purpose: action

Keep:
- logo placement
- footer treatment
- campaign marker
- type system

Change:
- CTA copy
- final supporting line
- optional final visual crop

Make this the cleanest slide, not the busiest.

---

# Build rules for the junior designer

Use these as hard instructions:

- Do **not** change the grid
- Do **not** introduce new fonts
- Do **not** add new colors outside the palette
- Do **not** move the logo
- Do **not** change image style between slides
- Do **not** invent new shapes or effects
- Do **not** alter spacing rules
- Do **not** redesign each slide independently

Every slide must feel like a variation of the same master template.

---

# Simple production formula

For every new post:
- keep the **locked system**
- swap the **message**
- swap the **visual**
- swap **one accent**

That’s it.

If you want, I can turn this into a **copy-paste template sheet** with:
- a fill-in-the-blank structure
- slide 1–5 content prompts
- and a designer handoff checklist.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is basically it. Clean enough to hand off, no wandering.

The only thing I’d tighten is the **campaign glue** — I’d probably make that even more explicit as a fixed trio:
- recurring placement
- recurring frame/shape
- recurring tone treatment

That’s the part junior people freestyle and ruin.

But overall, this is the kind of spec I wanted.
